title: An Arithmetic Refinement of Homological Mirror Symmetry for the 2-Torus
speaker: Yanki Lekili
abstract: We establish a derived equivalence of the Fukaya category of the 2-torus, relative to a basepoint, with the category of perfect complexes on the Tate curve over Z[q]. It specializes to an equivalence, over Z, of the Fukaya category of the punctured torus with perfect complexes on the nodal Weierstrass curve y^2+xy=x^3, and, over the punctured disc Z((q)), to an integral refinement of the known statement of homological mirror symmetry for the 2-torus. This is joint work with Tim Perutz.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic HMS statement over Z[[q]]
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the relative symplectic torus with basepoint and primitive, the Tate curve with its distinguished point and differential, and states the homological mirror symmetry equivalence over the integral power-series ring Z[[q]], emphasizing that this refines earlier Novikov-field versions.

proof_stages.label: Relative Fukaya category model
proof_stages.summary: The symplectic side is made concrete by describing exact embedded Lagrangian curves, Floer morphism complexes, holomorphic polygon products, and the q-weighting by intersection with the basepoint that makes the category defined over Z[[q]].

proof_stages.label: Čech DG model for perfect sheaves
proof_stages.summary: The algebraic side is modeled at chain level by vector bundles on the Tate curve, morphism complexes computed by Čech cochains for a fixed two-open affine cover, and then by passing to a triangulated closure modeling perfect complexes.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to two generators on each side
proof_stages.summary: Instead of comparing the full large categories directly, the proof restricts to generating subcategories: the two basic Lagrangians A and B on the torus and the objects O and O_sigma on the elliptic curve, so that a chain-level identification of these finite A∞ structures implies the HMS equivalence after triangulated closure.

proof_stages.label: Common cohomology algebra and transferred A∞ structures
proof_stages.summary: The Floer and Ext cohomology algebras of the two-generator subcategories are computed to be the same explicit finite algebra A, and homological perturbation transfers algebraic DG models for elliptic curves to A∞ structures on this fixed underlying algebra.

proof_stages.label: Deformation classification by Weierstrass curves
proof_stages.summary: General Weierstrass curves with their coordinate-change group action are mapped to the moduli of A∞ structures on A, and this map is proved to be an isomorphism by comparing deformation theories at the cusp/formal point via Hochschild cohomology computations.

proof_stages.label: Mirror-map computation identifying the Tate curve
proof_stages.summary: The symplectic A∞ structure gives a point in the Weierstrass/A∞ moduli space, and its corresponding curve is determined by reconstructing the homogeneous coordinate ring from the generator data and computing the required products by holomorphic triangle counts, yielding the explicit Tate curve.

examples.name: A nontrivial μ3 through the basepoint
examples.note: A previously computed μ3 product involving two Lagrangians and polygons hitting the basepoint is used to illustrate why higher Fukaya products on the torus are not merely combinatorial or automatic.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker cites a previously computed nontrivial μ3 through the basepoint as an illustration of hard higher products but gives no actual computation or verification in the talk

passes.reason: A nonzero μ3 through the basepoint is an ordinary higher Fukaya product used to exhibit a transversality obstruction.
A nonzero μ3 polygon hitting the basepoint is an ordinary higher Fukaya operation, used as an obstruction/example showing transversality and higher products are not automatically easy.
A nonzero ternary Fukaya product through the marked point is not structurally sporadic or extreme, but is used to show transversality and higher-product computations can fail to be easy.
A nonzero Fukaya-category μ3 operation through the basepoint is an ordinary higher product structurally, but it is used to exhibit the failure of transversality for higher products even on the torus.
An ordinary nonzero higher Fukaya product, used to exhibit failure of transversality in higher products.
a nonzero μ3 through the basepoint is an ordinary higher Fukaya operation used to exhibit transversality failure for higher products
It is used as an enemy case where higher-product transversality fails, while a nontrivial μ3 through the marked point is not intrinsically extremal, degenerate, symmetric, or sporadic.
A nontrivial μ3 through the basepoint is used as an obstruction showing higher products/transversality are not easy even on the torus.
Used as an obstruction to easy higher-product computations because it causes transversality failure, while structurally it is an ordinary nonzero Fukaya μ3 incidence with the basepoint.

used to exhibit a transversality failure/obstruction in computing higher Fukaya products, while the μ3 disk through the marked point is not intrinsically sporadic, extremal, degenerate, or maximally symmetric

used as an obstruction showing higher Fukaya products can be nontrivial and transversality can fail even on the torus, but the μ3 operation itself is not structurally exceptional, extremal, symmetric, or degenerate

Used as an obstruction to naive/easy computation of higher products because transversality fails at the basepoint, but structurally it is just a nonzero higher product rather than a classified exceptional, degenerate, extremal, or symmetric object.

examples.name: Infinite triangles for horizontal, vertical, and diagonal curves
examples.note: The universal-cover picture with horizontal, vertical, and diagonal Lagrangians shows infinitely many triangles with the same corners, motivating the replacement of area weights by basepoint-intersection powers of q.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker concretely analyzes the horizontal, vertical, and diagonal curves by identifying triangles with the same corners, noting infinitely many contributions, and explaining their weights by area/basepoint intersection

passes.reason: The infinite family of triangles for the standard three curves is used to show naive unweighted triangle counts fail and require Novikov/basepoint weights.

The infinite family of holomorphic triangles for the standard horizontal, vertical, and diagonal curves is structurally ordinary but is used to show naive unweighted triangle counts fail and require Novikov or basepoint weights.
The standard horizontal/vertical/diagonal triangle family is structurally ordinary, but its infinitely many contributing triangles obstruct naive unweighted counts.

The horizontal/vertical/diagonal triangle configuration is a standard torus Floer-count setup, but the infinite family of triangles is used as an obstruction to naive unweighted counts over the base ring.
A standard torus triangle-count configuration, used to show naive unweighted counts are infinite and need weighting.
the standard horizontal/vertical/diagonal triangle configuration is used to show naive triangle counts are infinite unless weighted
It is used to show that naive triangle counts can be infinite without Novikov or q-weighting, while the horizontal/vertical/diagonal torus curves form a standard systematic configuration.
The infinite family of triangles is used to show naive unweighted polygon counts fail and need Novikov or basepoint weighting.
Used to show naive holomorphic-triangle counts are infinite/ill-defined without weights, while the horizontal/vertical/diagonal curve configuration is a standard representative one.
used to show that naive triangle counts are infinite without Novikov/basepoint weights, while the horizontal-vertical-diagonal torus configuration is a standard non-exceptional one

used to show the naive triangle count is infinite and hence needs Novikov or basepoint weighting, while the horizontal/vertical/diagonal triangle family is a standard torus configuration rather than a structurally special object
Used to show the unweighted triangle count fails and needs Novikov/basepoint weights, and the infinitely many contributing triangles form an unbounded/extreme count rather than a finite typical one.
